title: What Are LLM Guardrails? Input and Output Validation for AI Apps
seoTitle: What Are LLM Guardrails? Validation for AI Apps
metaDescription: LLM guardrails are checks that run before and after every model call. What they catch — bad inputs, unsafe outputs, broken JSON — and how to start.
keywords: how to add guardrails to llm app
oneLiner: Understand what guardrails check before and after each model call, and how to add your first input and output validators.

blocks.md: **LLM guardrails** are the safety checks that run *around* a model call — one set on the way in, another on the way out. The guardrail on the input inspects what the user (or your own code) is about to send to the model. The guardrail on the output inspects what the model said before your app does anything with it. If either check fails, you block, fix, or retry instead of letting the bad thing through.

Here's the everyday analogy. Think of a nightclub with a bouncer at the door and a coat-check on the way out. The bouncer (input guardrail) decides who gets in — no fake IDs, no troublemakers, nobody trying to sneak in a weapon. The coat-check (output guardrail) makes sure that whatever leaves with you is actually yours and safe to carry out. The club itself — the LLM — is great at the party, but it has no judgment about the door. The guardrails supply that judgment.

Concretely, an input guardrail might reject an off-topic question, strip out a [prompt-injection](/learn/prompt-engineering/prompt-injection-security/what-is-prompt-injection) attempt, or refuse a message that's longer than your budget allows. An output guardrail might check that the model returned valid JSON, that it didn't leak a customer's email address, that it didn't recommend a competitor, or that it didn't confidently invent a refund policy. Guardrails are not part of the model — they're plain code (and sometimes a second, cheaper model) that you wrap around it.

A guardrail is a *deterministic gate*, not a vibe. It has a clear pass/fail rule and a clear action when it fails — block, rewrite, retry, or hand off to a human. That predictability is the whole point: the model is the unpredictable part, and the guardrail is the part you can reason about.

An LLM will happily produce text that is fluent, confident, and completely unusable. It can return prose where you asked for JSON, an apology where you asked for an answer, a made-up citation, a slur, or a string that — passed straight to your database or shell — does real damage. The model has no built-in contract with your app. Guardrails are how you bolt that contract on.

The core problem guardrails solve is that **you cannot trust raw model output, and you cannot trust raw user input either.** Normal software validates its inputs and checks its outputs as a matter of habit — you'd never write user text straight into a SQL query. LLM apps tempt you to skip that step because the output *looks* finished. Guardrails restore the discipline: treat the model like an untrusted external service on both sides of the call.

items: **Anyone whose model output feeds another system** — a database write, a function call, an email send. A malformed or malicious string here becomes a real bug or a real breach.

**Teams handling sensitive data** — PII, health, finance. An output guardrail that catches a leaked Social Security number before it reaches a screen is the difference between a near-miss and an incident.

**Anyone building [agents](/learn/ai-agents/agent-fundamentals/what-is-an-ai-agent)** — an agent that calls tools on its own needs guardrails on every step, because a bad decision doesn't just print, it *acts*.
**Customer-facing products** — brand-safety, refusal of off-topic or abusive requests, and staying on-policy all live in guardrails.

blocks.md: What did guardrails replace? Mostly *hoping the prompt was good enough*. The old approach was to stuff every rule into the system prompt — "never reveal secrets, always return JSON, stay polite" — and pray the model obeyed every time. Prompts are a real defense and worth doing well (see [prompt engineering](/learn/prompt-engineering/prompting-basics/what-is-prompt-engineering)), but a prompt is a *request*, not a *guarantee*. Guardrails are the enforcement layer that runs whether or not the model cooperated.

The most dangerous failures pass typecheck and look successful: a well-formatted answer that happens to contain a customer's phone number, or a clean JSON object whose `amount` field is negative. Guardrails exist to catch the failures that *don't* throw an error on their own.

A guardrail sits at a choke point in your request path. The input guardrail runs before the model call; the output guardrail runs after. Each one inspects the text, applies a rule, and decides: pass it through, fix it, or reject it. Nothing reaches the model — or the user — until the relevant gate says yes.

diagram.title: Where guardrails sit in a request

blocks.md: Guardrails come in a few flavors depending on how they make the decision. Some are simple deterministic code; some call a model or an ML classifier to judge fuzzier things. You mix them based on what you're checking:

rows: Email or card numbers, banned words, max length
Broken JSON, missing fields, wrong types, bad ranges
A small ML model scores the text
A second model grades the first
Faithfulness to sources, tone, policy nuance

blocks.md: The deterministic ones (rule and schema) are fast, free, and 100% predictable — always your first line of defense. The model-based ones ([classifier](/learn/multimodal-ai/vision-models/what-is-vision-language-model) and [LLM-as-judge](/learn/evaluation-safety/llm-as-judge/llm-as-a-judge-explained)) handle the fuzzy stuff a regex can't, at the cost of extra latency and money. A good guardrail stack uses cheap deterministic checks first and only reaches for a model when the question genuinely needs judgment.
When a guardrail fails, it has to *do* something. The common failure actions form a ladder from gentle to hard:

diagram.title: What a failed guardrail can do

layers.label: Fix
layers.sub: redact the PII, coerce to the schema, keep going

layers.label: Retry
layers.sub: re-prompt the model, often with the error

layers.label: Fallback
layers.sub: return a safe canned answer or a cheaper model

layers.label: Block
layers.sub: refuse, escalate to a human, log it

blocks.md: Which action you pick depends on the stakes. A broken-JSON output is usually worth a silent retry. A leaked secret should be redacted *and* logged. An abusive input should be blocked outright. The art of guardrails is choosing the lightest action that still keeps the bad thing from reaching anyone who matters.

You don't need a framework to start — you need two small functions wrapped around your model call. Here's the smallest useful pair: an input guardrail that rejects over-long messages and an output guardrail that forces the answer into a typed schema, retrying once if the model returns garbage. The schema check uses [Pydantic](https://pydantic.dev/docs/validation/latest/get-started/), the standard data-validation library in Python — the same trick that powers most [structured-output](/learn/llm-apis/streaming-structured-outputs/llm-streaming-explained) helpers.

blocks.title: guardrails.py

blocks.md: That's a complete guardrail loop in under 40 lines. The input check blocks an abusive payload *before* you pay for a single token. The output check guarantees that whatever your app receives is a real `Refund` object with a non-negative amount — never a freeform paragraph, never a negative number. And the retry feeds the validation error back to the model, which is often enough for it to fix its own mistake on the second try.

Always pass the failure reason back into the retry prompt. "Your last reply was invalid: amount cannot be negative" gives the model a concrete fix; a bare "try again" makes it guess. Self-correcting retries are one of the highest-leverage guardrail patterns there is.

Beginners constantly confuse **guardrails** with **evals**, because both are about quality. The difference is *when* they run and *what they protect*. Guardrails run live, on every single request, and protect the *current* user from a single bad response. [Evals](/learn/evaluation-safety/evaluation-basics/what-are-llm-evals) run offline, on a fixed test set, and tell you whether your app *as a whole* got better or worse after a change.

columns.title: Guardrails
items: Must be fast — they're in the hot path

columns.title: Evals
items: Run offline, on a test set
Measure the app over many cases

blocks.md: They work as a team. An eval might reveal that 4% of your answers leak PII; that finding tells you to *add a PII guardrail*. Later, a guardrail blocks a real leak in production; you turn that real example into a *new eval case* so a future change can't reintroduce it. Guardrails catch the live miss, evals prove the fix generalizes, and the loop feeds [testing](/learn/production-llmops/testing-deployment/testing-llm-apps) and [observability](/learn/production-llmops/observability-monitoring/what-is-llm-observability) alike. Neither replaces the other.

Rule of thumb: if it has to protect a real user *right now*, it's a guardrail. If it tells you whether the system is good *in general*, it's an eval. You want both, and every guardrail failure you see in production is a free new eval case.

You can hand-roll guardrails like the example above, and for many apps that's the right call — a few regexes and a Pydantic schema cover most of what you need. When you want pre-built validators for harder problems (toxicity, PII detection, prompt-injection scoring), a couple of open-source frameworks package those up:

items: **Guardrails AI** — a Python framework with a *Hub* of ready-made input/output validators you compose into guards. Strong at structured-output enforcement and risk detection; runs as a library or a standalone server.

**NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails** — a toolkit focused on conversational systems, with a small modeling language (*Colang*) for defining what the bot may and may not do. Splits rails into input, dialog, retrieval, execution, and output stages.
**Pydantic** — not a guardrail framework, but the workhorse for the *schema* half of the job. If your only guardrail is "the output must match this typed shape," Pydantic alone gets you there.
**Provider-side filters** — most model APIs ship their own safety classifiers and refusal behavior. Treat these as a baseline, not your whole defense — they don't know your app's policies.

blocks.md: Start hand-rolled, reach for a framework when you need its validators. The frameworks save you from rewriting a good PII detector or toxicity classifier — but the *placement* (input vs output) and the *failure action* (block vs retry) are decisions only you can make, framework or not.
Once basic input and output checks are in place, a harder set of guardrail problems shows up — the ones that separate a demo-grade safeguard from a production one.

The clean retry pattern above assumes you have the *whole* answer before you check it. But many apps [stream](/learn/llm-apis/streaming-structured-outputs/llm-streaming-explained) tokens to the user as they arrive, which means a banned word or a leaked secret may already be on screen before your output guardrail ever sees the full text. Production systems handle this with *partial-output* checks: validating chunks as they stream, buffering risky spans, or running a fast classifier on a short trailing window. It's a genuine tension — streaming feels instant, but instant output is output you haven't validated yet.

blocks.text: Guardrails for agents and tool calls

blocks.md: A chatbot's worst output is bad text. An [agent](/learn/ai-agents/tool-use/what-is-tool-use) that can run code, send emails, or move money has a worse worst case: a bad *action*. So agent guardrails wrap the [tool call](/learn/llm-apis/function-calling/what-is-function-calling), not just the text — allow-lists of permitted tools, argument validation before execution, spend or rate caps, and human-in-the-loop approval for anything irreversible. The guardrail moves from "is this string safe to show?" to "is this action safe to take?", which is a higher bar.

blocks.text: Adversarial inputs and the limits of guardrails

blocks.md: Attackers actively try to slip past guardrails — a [prompt injection](/learn/prompt-engineering/prompt-injection-security/what-is-prompt-injection) hidden in a retrieved document, a jailbreak phrased to dodge your classifier, Unicode tricks that defeat a naive regex. This is the domain of [red-teaming](/learn/evaluation-safety/red-teaming-jailbreaks/what-is-ai-red-teaming): deliberately attacking your own app to find the gaps. The sober truth is that no single guardrail is bulletproof, which is why serious systems layer them — defense in depth — and assume any one layer can fail.

Every guardrail you add costs time and sometimes money, and they sit in the hot path where users feel the delay. A regex is free; an LLM-as-judge on every output can double your latency and your bill. Mature setups order guardrails cheapest-first (bail out early on a fast deterministic check), run independent model-based checks in parallel, and reserve the expensive judge for high-stakes paths only. Guardrails are a safety/latency trade-off — the goal is maximum protection for the smallest budget, not a check on every conceivable risk.

The mature mental model: **guardrails are a layered, living system, not a one-time wrapper.** New failure modes appear, attackers adapt, and policies change — so your guardrails grow alongside your [evals](/learn/evaluation-safety/evaluation-basics/what-are-llm-evals) and your [LLMOps](/learn/production-llmops/llmops-fundamentals/what-is-llmops) practice. Treated that way, they're what lets you put an LLM feature in front of real users and sleep at night.

faq.q: What are LLM guardrails in simple terms?
faq.a: LLM guardrails are safety checks that run before and after a model call. The input guardrail inspects what you send to the model; the output guardrail inspects what the model returns before your app uses it. If a check fails, you block, fix, or retry instead of letting the bad input or output through.

faq.q: What's the difference between input and output guardrails?
faq.a: Input guardrails run before the model call and catch bad or unsafe requests — over-long messages, off-topic questions, prompt-injection attempts. Output guardrails run after the call and catch bad responses — broken JSON, leaked PII, off-policy or toxic text. Most production apps use both.

faq.q: How do I add guardrails to my LLM app?
faq.a: Start small and hand-rolled: a function that validates input (length, allowed topics) before the call, and a schema check (e.g. with Pydantic) on the output after the call, retrying once with the error fed back if it fails. Add a framework like Guardrails AI or NeMo Guardrails only when you need pre-built validators for things like toxicity or PII.

faq.q: Are guardrails the same as prompt engineering?
faq.a: No. A prompt is a request to the model — it asks for good behavior but can't guarantee it. A guardrail is enforcement code that runs whether or not the model obeyed the prompt. Good apps use both: a strong prompt to encourage the right output, and a guardrail to verify it actually happened.

faq.q: What's the difference between guardrails and evals?
faq.a: Guardrails run live on every request and protect the current user from one bad response. Evals run offline on a fixed test set and measure whether your app as a whole got better or worse. They feed each other — a guardrail failure in production becomes a new eval case so the bug can't return.

faq.q: Do guardrails slow down my app?
faq.a: Deterministic guardrails like regex and schema checks are effectively free. Model-based ones — a toxicity classifier or an LLM-as-judge — add real latency and cost because they make an extra call. The fix is ordering: run cheap checks first, run model-based checks in parallel, and reserve the expensive ones for high-stakes paths.
